channel.title: NYT > Obituaries
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Country Joe McDonald, Whose Antiwar Song Became an Anthem, Dies at 84
item.description: One of the starring acts at Woodstock, he and his band, the Fish, came out of the Bay Area’s psychedelic rock scene. He went on to a long career as a solo artist.
item.dc:creator: Jim Farber
item.category: Country Joe and the Fish (Music Group)
item.media:description: Country Joe McDonald in 1981. The tone of the politics and social commentary in his songs could range from whimsical to snarky.

item.title: Tatjana Wood, Award-Winning Comic Book Colorist, Dies at 99
item.description: She was part of the acclaimed creative teams on comic book series for DC Comics, including Swamp Thing, which she called “Shvampy” in her German accent.
item.dc:creator: George Gene Gustines

item.title: Thaddeus Mosley, Sculptor Who Found Fame in His Last Decade, Dies at 99
item.description: A self-taught artist, he turned reclaimed wood into striking abstract works influenced by Brancusi, Noguchi and African art.
item.dc:creator: Will Heinrich
item.media:credit: Ross Mantle for The New York Times
item.media:description: Thaddeus Mosley at his studio in Pittsburgh in 2023. Of the racial and economic barriers he faced, he said: “I knew that I wasn’t going to get the same recognition, have the same opportunity. But I tried, because it’s something I wanted to do.”

item.title: Jeremy Larner, Who Wrote ‘The Candidate,’ a Political Film Classic, Dies at 88
item.description: His Oscar-winning 1972 screenplay starred Robert Redford as an idealistic public interest lawyer making a run for the Senate.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir

item.title: António Lobo Antunes, One of Europe’s Most Revered Writers, Dies at 83
item.description: In a career studded with literary awards, he was the author of dozens of books that grappled with his nation’s legacy of dictatorship and colonialism.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter
item.media:description: António Lobo Antunes in 1988. The literary critic George Steiner called him “a novelist of the very first rank” and likened him to Joseph Conrad and William Faulkner.

item.title: Ronnie Eldridge, NY Politician and Aide to RFK and Lindsay, Dies at 95
item.description: She was an adviser to Senator Robert F. Kennedy, Mayor John V. Lindsay and Representative Bella S. Abzug before serving on the New York City Council from 1989 to 2001.
item.dc:creator: Sam Roberts
item.media:credit: Marilynn K. Yee/The New York Times
item.media:description: Ronnie Eldridge at a City Council meeting in 1991. Two decades earlier, while working as a top aide to Mayor John V. Lindsay, she was described by The Times as “one of the most influential women of politics in the city government.”

item.title: Juan Jose Valdez, Last Marine Out of Saigon, Dies at 88
item.description: During the fall of the South Vietnamese capital in 1975, he helped evacuate thousands, and was nearly left behind.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces

item.title: Lou Holtz, Who Coached Unbeaten Notre Dame to a Title, Dies at 89
item.description: Known for reviving football programs, he led six major colleges to bowl games, winning a national championship in 1989 after restoring the Irish to greatness.
item.dc:creator: Richard Goldstein
item.media:description: Lou Holtz with Notre Dame players in about 1988. Unassuming in demeanor, he had a keen football mind and a disciplinarian’s resolve.

item.title: Alan Trustman, Who Wrote ‘Bullitt’ and ‘Thomas Crown Affair,’ Dies at 95
item.description: In a wide-ranging career, he was a Boston lawyer, a Hollywood screenwriter and a Swiss currency trader.
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams

item.title: From 1999: Charlotte Perriand, Designer, Is Dead at 96
item.description: Le Corbusier famously told her, “We don’t embroider cushions here,” when she sought a job at his studio. Then he recognized her talent for design.
item.dc:creator: Julie V. Iovine

item.title: From 2001: Aaliyah, 22, Singer Who First Hit the Charts at 14
item.description: She was seen as a hip-hop temptress when she was still a teenager, and her albums “Age Ain’t Nothing but a Number” and “One in a Million” sold millions of copies.
item.dc:creator: Jon Pareles
item.media:description: Aaliyah in 1995.

item.title: From 1978: Golda Meir, 80, Dies in Jerusalem; Israelis Acclaim ‘Stalwart Lioness’
item.description: Once a teacher in Milwaukee, she climbed the ranks of the Labor Party to become Israel’s first female prime minister.
item.dc:creator: Paul Hoffman and Special to The New York Times

item.title: From 2014: Maya Angelou, Lyrical Witness of the Jim Crow South, Dies at 86
item.description: Her landmark book “I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings” was among the first 20th-century autobiographies of a Black woman to reach a wide readership.
item.dc:creator: Margalit Fox

item.title: From 2002: Sylvia Rivera, Figure in Birth of the Gay Liberation Movement, Dies at 50
item.description: When patrons at the Stonewall Inn faced down a police raid, she shouted to her lover, “I’m not missing a minute of this — it’s the revolution!”
item.dc:creator: David W. Dunlap

item.title: From 1983: Lise Meitner, Physicist, Is Dead at 89; Paved Way for Splitting of Atom
item.description: She laid much of the theoretical groundwork for the atomic bomb, although she did not participate directly in its production.
item.dc:creator: Special to The New York Times
item.media:description: Lise Meitner in an undated photo with the German nuclear chemist Otto Hahn.

item.title: From 2001: Beate Uhse, 81, Entrepreneur in the Business of Erotic Goods
item.description: She developed Europe’s biggest emporium of erotic goods and opened a three-story sex museum in Germany.
item.dc:creator: Wolfgang Saxon
item.media:description: Beate Uhse in 1971, in front of her mail-order company headquarters in Flensburg, Germany.

item.title: From 2011: Wangari Maathai, Nobel Peace Prize Laureate, Dies at 71
item.description: A Kenyan environmentalist, she began by paying women a few shillings to plant trees and went on to become the first African woman to win a Nobel Peace Prize.
item.dc:creator: Jeffrey Gettleman
item.media:description: Wangari Maathai in 2005, with a tree she planted in the Newlands Forest in Cape Town, South Africa.

item.title: Christine Keeler, Central Figure in British ‘Scandal of the Century,’ Is Dead at 75
item.description: News of her affair with the government minister John Profumo and other revelations set England abuzz in the early 1960s.
item.dc:creator: Neil Genzlinger

item.title: From 2010: Victoria Manalo Draves, Olympic Champion Diver, Dies at 85
item.description: She overcame prejudice early in her career to become the first woman to win a gold medal in both the springboard and the platform, at the 1948 Olympic Games in London.
item.dc:creator: Frank Litsky

item.title: From 2014: Alice Coachman, 90, Dies; First Black Woman to Win Olympic Gold
item.description: After capturing the high jump for the United States at the 1948 London Games, she returned home to a segregated South.
item.dc:creator: Richard Goldstein
item.media:description: Alice Coachman in 1948 at Wembley Stadium in London, after winning the high jump at the Olympics.

item.title: From 2004: Fanny Blankers-Koen, Star of ’48 Olympics, Dies at 85
item.description: A Dutch housewife, she emulated her hero, Jesse Owens, by setting an Olympic record in track and field.
item.dc:creator: Frank Litsky
item.media:description: Fanny Blankers-Koen crossing the finish line of the 200-meter event in London at the 1948 Olympic Games.

item.title: From 1998: Florence Griffith Joyner, 38, Champion Sprinter, Is Dead
item.description: She revolutionized women’s sprinting with her speed and flamboyant fashion sense, and was a three-time gold medalist at the 1988 Summer Olympics.
item.dc:creator: Jeré Longman
item.media:credit: Tony Duffy/Allsport, via Getty Images, via Hulton Archive, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Florence Griffith Joyner displaying her medals from the Summer Olympic Games in Seoul in 1988.

item.title: From 1996: Toni Stone, 75, First Woman to Play Big-League Baseball
item.description: She was hired as a novelty by the Negro League’s Indianapolis Clowns, but went on to win recognition for her historic career.
item.dc:creator: Robert McG. Thomas Jr.

item.title: From 1956: Babe Zaharias Dies; Athlete Had Cancer
item.description: From the time she made the headlines during the 1932 Olympic Games at Los Angeles, she reigned as the world’s top all-around female athlete.
item.dc:creator: Special to The New York Times
item.media:description: Babe Zaharias during a golf tournament in 1950.

item.title: From 1994: Wilma Rudolph, Star of the 1960 Olympics, Dies at 54
item.description: She won three gold medals as a sprinter in the Games, an achievement that seemed unlikely given her poor health as a child.
item.dc:creator: Frank Litsky
item.media:description: Ms. Rudolph during the women’s 200-meter sprint at the 1960 Olympic Games in Rome.

item.title: From 2003: Althea Gibson, First Black Wimbledon Champion, Dies at 76
item.description: She grew up far from the genteel world of tennis, but became a pre-eminent figure in the women’s sport, reigning as queen of the lawns at Wimbledon and Forest Hills.
item.dc:creator: Robert McG. Thomas Jr.
item.media:description: Althea Gibson wins Wimbledon!

item.title: From 1983: Karen Carpenter, 32, Is Dead; Singer Teamed With Brother
item.description: The Carpenters sold more than 30 million records with the irresistible combination of her soft-rock contralto and her brother’s lush arrangements.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:description: Karen Carpenter performing around 1975.

item.title: From 2007: Anna Nicole Smith Dies at 39
item.description: A former Playboy centerfold, actress and TV personality, she was also known for being rich (sporadically) and litigious (chronically).
item.dc:creator: Abby Goodnough and Margalit Fox
item.media:description: Anna Nicole Smith in 1993.

item.title: From 1995: Grammy-Winning Singer Selena Killed in Shooting at Texas Motel
item.description: Known as the queen of Tejano music, she was beloved as an idol and a heartthrob on both sides of the Mexican border.
item.dc:creator: Sam Howe Verhovek
item.media:credit: Dave Einsel/Houston Chronicle, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Selena performing in Houston in 1993.

item.title: From 1962: Brilliant Stardom and Personal Tragedy Punctuated the Life of Marilyn Monroe
item.description: One of the most famous stars in Hollywood, she suffered severe setbacks in the last years of her life.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: From 1981: Death Ends a 39-Year Career, Starting With Child Roles at 4
item.description: Natalie Wood evolved from a child star into a teenage ingénue and then a mature actress, until her trajectory was tragically cut short.
item.dc:creator: Robert D. McFadden
item.media:description: Natalie Wood around 1969.

item.title: From 2007: Benazir Bhutto, 54, Who Weathered Pakistan’s Political Storm for 3 Decades, Dies
item.description: She was the first democratically elected woman to lead a modern Muslim country.
item.dc:creator: John F. Burns

item.title: From 1984: Indira Gandhi, Born to Politics, Left Her Own Imprint on India
item.description: The dominant figure in Indian politics for nearly two decades, she was elected prime minister four times before being assassinated by her bodyguards.
item.dc:creator: William K. Stevens and Special to The New York Times
item.media:description: Indira Gandhi in 1963. She served as India’s premier from 1966 to 1974, and was elected again in 1980.

item.title: From 2000: Sirimavo Bandaranaike of Sri Lanka Dies at 84; First Woman Premier
item.description: She rose to power in 1960 as a widow and mother of three, becoming the first woman in the world to serve as a prime minister.
item.dc:creator: Celia W. Dugger
item.media:description: Sirimavo Bandaranaike in 1961, speaking with Duncan Sandys, the British secretary of state for commonwealth relations.

item.title: From 1997: Mother Teresa, Hope of the Despairing, Dies at 87
item.description: She won the Nobel Peace Prize in 1979 for providing care and comfort to the impoverished.
item.dc:creator: Eric Pace
item.media:description: Mother Teresa around 1990. She set up welfare institutions, including a family clinic, mobile leprosy clinics and nurseries for abandoned children.

item.title: From 2013: Margaret Thatcher, ‘Iron Lady’ Who Set Britain on New Course, Dies at 87
item.description: She held the office of prime minister longer than any other British politician in the 20th century, setting her country on a right-leaning economic path.
item.dc:creator: Joseph R. Gregory
item.media:description: Margaret Thatcher in 1986. She was the first woman to become prime minister of Britain and the first to lead a major Western power in modern times.

item.title: From 2007: Vilma Espín, Rebel and Wife of Raúl Castro, Dies at 77
item.description: An idealistic socialite who fought alongside Fidel and Raúl Castro in the mountains of Cuba, she was the country’s unofficial first lady for decades.
item.dc:creator: Anthony DePalma
item.media:description: Vilma Espín around 1959. She became a widely recognized symbol of the Castro family’s half-century grip on power.

item.title: From 2006: Coretta Scott King, a Civil Rights Icon, Dies at 78
item.description: First known as the wife of the Rev. Dr. Martin Luther King Jr., she later became an avid proselytizer for his vision of peace and nonviolent social change.
item.dc:creator: Peter Applebome
item.media:description: Coretta Scott King in 1986.

item.title: From 1989: Christine Jorgensen, 62, Is Dead; Was First to Have a Sex Change
item.description: Her gender conversion began with hormone injections in 1950. On her return to the U.S. in 1955, she was sensationalized in the tabloids.
item.dc:creator: John T. McQuiston
item.media:credit: Library of Congress/Corbis/VCG, via Getty Images
item.media:description: “At first I was very self-conscious and very awkward,” Christine Jorgensen said of her gender transition. “But once the notoriety hit, it did not take me long to adjust.”

item.title: From 1992: Marlene Dietrich, 90, Symbol of Glamour, Dies
item.description: “Dietrich is something that never existed before and may never exist again,” the actor Maurice Chevalier said of her. “That’s a woman.”
item.dc:creator: Peter B. Flint
item.media:credit: Eugene Robert Richee/John Kobal Foundation, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Marlene Dietrich in 1935. She was an international symbol of glamour and sex for more than half a century.

item.title: From 1973: Mrs. Marjorie Merriweather Post Is Dead at 86
item.description: The owner of General Foods Corporation, she was a generous philanthropist whose great wealth also allowed her to entertain lavishly and live regally.
item.dc:creator: Special to The New York Times
item.media:description: Marjorie Merriweather Post in 1957 at her home in Washington.

item.title: From 2004: Estée Lauder, Pursuer of Beauty and Cosmetics Titan, Dies at 97
item.description: Her company, known for its creams, powders, ointments and perfumes, was estimated to be worth about $5 billion when it went public in 1995.
item.dc:creator: Richard Severo
item.media:description: Estée Lauder at her office in 1985. She convinced generations of women determined to maintain a youthful appearance that her beauty creams were “jars of hope.”

item.title: From 1935: Jane Addams Dies in Her 76th Year
item.description: A world-famous social welfare worker, she won a Nobel Prize for her efforts on behalf of world peace.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations

item.title: From 2019: Toni Morrison, Towering Novelist of the Black Experience, Dies at 88
item.description: Ms. Morrison, who wrote “Beloved” and “Song of Solomon,” was the first African-American woman to win the Nobel in literature.
item.dc:creator: Margalit Fox
item.media:description: Toni Morrison in 2008.

item.title: From 1969: Judy Garland, 47, Found Dead
item.description: She dazzled audiences in “The Wizard of Oz” and “A Star Is Born,” but her successes were later overshadowed by addiction and other struggles.
item.dc:creator: Special to The New York Times
item.media:description: Judy Garland around 1941.

item.title: From 2004: Julia Child, the French Chef for a Jell-O Nation, Dies at 91
item.description: She was a towering figure on the culinary front for more than 40 years, turning the art of French cooking into prime-time television entertainment.
item.dc:creator: Regina Schrambling
item.media:credit: ZUMA Press Wire, via Reuters Connect
item.media:description: Julia Child in 1978. “She demystified French cuisine in a way that had not been done before, in an appealing, straightforward way,” the chef Jacques Pépin said.

item.title: From 1998: Martha Gellhorn, Daring Writer, Dies at 89
item.description: One of the first female war correspondents, she covered a dozen major conflicts during a career that spanned more than six decades.
item.dc:creator: Rick Lyman

item.title: From 1922: Nellie Bly, Journalist, Dies of Pneumonia
item.description: She was best known for her trip around the world in 1889, which she completed in 72 days 6 hours 11 minutes.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:description: Nellie Bly in 1950. During the course of her career, she not only traveled around the world in less than 80 days, she also went down into the ocean in a diving bell, went up in the air in a balloon and lived in a mental institution as a patient.

item.title: From 1977: Maria Callas, 53, Is Dead of Heart Attack in Paris
item.description: Considered the most exciting opera singer of her time, she thrilled audiences with her penchant for spectacle onstage and in her personal life.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Ericson
item.media:description: Maria Callas in the 2017 documentary “Maria By Callas,” directed by Tom Volf.

item.title: From 2005: Ruth Clement Bond, 101, Quilter and Civic Leader, Is Dead
item.description: She helped transform the American quilt from a utilitarian bed covering into a work of avant-garde social commentary.
item.dc:creator: Margalit Fox

item.title: From 1994: Jacqueline Kennedy Onassis Dies of Cancer at 64
item.description: She became an object of fascination to generations of Americans after her husband, President John F. Kennedy, was assassinated in 1963.
item.dc:creator: Robert D. McFadden

item.title: From 1962: Mrs. Roosevelt Dies at 78 After Illness of Six Weeks
item.description: The death of the former first lady, a noted humanitarian and an active force in the Democratic Party, was mourned by people around the world.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:description: Eleanor Roosevelt at the 1956 Democratic National Convention in Chicago.

item.title: From 2010: Lucille Clifton, Poet Who Explored Black Lives, Dies at 73
item.description: A distinguished American poet, she examined the experience of being Black and female in the 20th century.
item.dc:creator: Margalit Fox
item.media:credit: Afro American Newspapers/Gado, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Lucille Clifton in 1995.

item.title: From 1967: Dorothy Parker, Literary Wit, Dies at Age 73
item.description: She enjoyed a lifelong reputation as a glittering, annihilating humorist. For her epitaph, she suggested, “Excuse My Dust.”
item.dc:creator: Alden Whitman
item.media:description: Dorothy Parker in 1941. Her writing often appeared in The New Yorker, starting with the magazine’s second issue, on Feb. 28, 1925.

item.title: From 1992: Audre Lorde, 58, a Poet, Memoirist and Lecturer, Dies
item.description: Her large body of work, which included poetry, essays and autobiography, reflected her hatred of racial and sexual prejudice.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:description: Audre Lorde in 1983. Six years later, her collection of essays, “A Burst of Light,” won an American Book Award.

item.title: From 1959: Billie Holiday Dies Here at 44; Jazz Singer Had Wide Influence
item.description: Miss Holiday, who became a singer more out of desperation than desire, was one of the most influential jazz musicians of her time.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:description: Billie Holiday performing in Manhattan in 1947.

item.title: From 2011: Amy Winehouse, British Soul Singer With a Troubled Life, Dies at 27
item.description: A British singer who found worldwide fame with her sassy, hip-hop-inflected take on retro soul, she became a tabloid fixture because of addiction problems.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario
item.media:description: Amy Winehouse sold millions of albums and won five Grammy Awards.

item.title: From 2019: Agnès Varda, Influential French New Wave Filmmaker, Dies at 90
item.description: She was closely associated with the film movement known as the New Wave, although her reimagining of cinematic conventions predated it.
item.dc:creator: John Anderson
